h1Title: Acanthosis Nigricans: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Acanthosis nigricans is a dermatological marker characterized by dark, velvety, thickened plaques occurring in skin folds and body creases — most commonly the posterior neck, axillae, and groin — caused by epidermal hyperplasia driven by excess insulin or other growth-factor receptor signaling. It affects an estimated 7-74% of obese adults and children in the United States, with prevalence strongly correlated with degree of insulin resistance. In the overwhelming majority of cases it signals underlying insulin resistance, prediabetes, type 2 diabetes, obesity, or polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS). A rare but clinically critical form — malignant acanthosis nigricans — is paraneoplastic, most commonly associated with gastric adenocarcinoma, and demands urgent oncological investigation. Treatment addresses the underlying cause: weight loss and insulin-sensitizing therapies improve the skin lesions in metabolic forms; the skin changes of malignant acanthosis nigricans regress only with effective cancer treatment.

keyStats.prevalence: Prevalence 7-74% in obese adults depending on population studied; 40-50% in Hispanic and African-American children with obesity (Guran 2008); 66-80% in adults with type 2 diabetes and BMI >35
keyStats.demographics: Highest rates in Hispanic, African-American, and Native American populations compared with white populations at equivalent BMI; affects all ages from infancy to elderly
keyStats.avgAge: Metabolic form most common in adolescence and young adulthood; malignant form peaks in adults over 40
keyStats.globalCases: No precise global case count; parallels the global obesity and type 2 diabetes epidemics — estimated hundreds of millions at risk

definition: Acanthosis nigricans (ICD-10: L83) is a non-inflammatory skin disorder characterized by symmetrical, hyperpigmented, velvety hyperplasia of the epidermis occurring in flexural skin creases and body folds. The underlying pathophysiology involves binding of insulin — when present at high concentrations — to insulin-like growth factor-1 (IGF-1) receptors on keratinocytes, driving keratinocyte and fibroblast proliferation and resulting in the characteristic thickened, papillomatous, pigmented epidermis. The same hyperplastic response occurs with signaling through epidermal growth factor receptors in drug-induced forms and paraneoplastic secretion of IGF-1, transforming growth factor-alpha, or other growth factors from malignant tumors. The condition is classified by etiology into: benign acanthosis nigricans (insulin resistance–associated, the dominant form), syndromic forms (fibroblast growth factor receptor mutations, PCOS, Cushing's syndrome), drug-induced forms (systemic corticosteroids, nicotinic acid, oral contraceptives, HIV protease inhibitors), and malignant acanthosis nigricans (paraneoplastic, rare). Dermatorhology and endocrinology jointly manage the condition when metabolic causes are identified. The skin findings themselves are not harmful but carry critical importance as a visible biomarker of systemic metabolic disease.

typesClassification.description: The dominant form, occurring in individuals with hyperinsulinemia from obesity, prediabetes, type 2 diabetes, or polycystic ovary syndrome. Severity correlates with fasting insulin level and BMI. Skin changes improve with insulin sensitization and weight loss.

Rare but clinically urgent form associated with occult malignancy, classically gastric adenocarcinoma (70% of cases) but also other adenocarcinomas. Onset is rapid; involvement is extensive, including the mucous membranes, palms, and soles (tripe palms). The skin lesion typically precedes the cancer diagnosis and regresses with successful tumor treatment.

Caused by agents that raise insulin levels or directly stimulate growth-factor receptors: systemic corticosteroids, nicotinic acid (niacin), insulin injections at high dose, oral contraceptives, HIV protease inhibitors, and systemic retinoid derivatives. Typically reversible on drug withdrawal.

Associated with syndromes of severe insulin resistance (type A and B insulin resistance syndromes, leprechaunism, Rabson-Mendenhall syndrome), fibroblast growth factor receptor mutations (Crouzon and Apert syndromes), lipodystrophy, and PCOS with hyperandrogenism. Often presents in childhood or adolescence.

A rare benign variant without metabolic or neoplastic association, typically following Blaschko's lines on the trunk. Considered a mosaic form. No systemic investigation required in the absence of other risk factors.

primarySymptoms: Dark brown to black velvety thickening of the posterior neck — often first noticed as a 'dirty neck' that does not respond to washing — the most common and visible location, present in over 80% of cases.
Similar dark, velvety, thickened plaques in the axillae (armpits), appearing bilaterally and symmetrically, often with fine surface papillomatosis giving a warty or velvety texture.
Involvement of the groin, inguinal creases, and inner thighs, particularly in obese individuals where skin-fold friction and hyperinsulinemia combine.
Perioral involvement (lips, oral mucosa) in malignant acanthosis nigricans — mucosal involvement is an important clinical red flag demanding urgent oncological screening.
Palmar and plantar hyperkeratosis ('tripe palms' — ridged, velvety palmar thickening) accompanying malignant acanthosis nigricans, rarely seen in benign forms.
Skin-tag formation (acrochordon) in the axillae and neck, frequently co-occurring with acanthosis nigricans as they share the same insulin-driven keratinocyte proliferation mechanism.
Mild pruritus or discomfort in affected flexural areas, particularly in the axillae and groin where friction and moisture compound the thickened skin.
Gradual darkening and thickening over months to years in the metabolic form — rapid onset over weeks suggests the malignant paraneoplastic form and requires urgent investigation.
Involvement of the antecubital and popliteal fossae, periumbilical area, and beneath the breasts in more extensive metabolic acanthosis nigricans.
No associated skin ulceration, erosion, or active inflammation — the skin is benign in texture; roughening and pigmentation are the dominant features without pain at rest.
earlyWarningSigns: Dark velvety discoloration of the posterior neck in an overweight child or adolescent — one of the strongest visible early markers of insulin resistance before fasting glucose becomes abnormal.
New onset axillary darkening and skin-tag formation in a young woman with irregular periods — a cluster strongly suggesting PCOS with hyperinsulinemia.

Rapid-onset extensive acanthosis nigricans with mucosal involvement in an adult without obesity or metabolic disease — the pattern of malignant acanthosis nigricans demands urgent investigation for occult cancer.
emergencySigns: Rapid onset of extensive acanthosis nigricans involving mucous membranes, palms, and soles in a non-obese adult over 40 — urgent investigation for occult malignancy, particularly gastric adenocarcinoma, is required.
Acanthosis nigricans with weight loss, anorexia, and abdominal symptoms — the combination is classic for paraneoplastic presentation of gastrointestinal adenocarcinoma.

New onset acanthosis nigricans with diabetic ketoacidosis symptoms (extreme thirst, frequent urination, nausea, abdominal pain) — emergency endocrine evaluation for newly diagnosed type 2 or type 1 diabetes.

causes.description: High circulating insulin levels — from obesity, prediabetes, type 2 diabetes, or PCOS — bind to IGF-1 receptors on keratinocytes and fibroblasts at supraphysiological concentrations. This triggers dose-dependent epidermal proliferation and papillomatosis producing the characteristic skin change. Acanthosis nigricans severity correlates directly with fasting insulin levels (r=0.58, PMID 12634985) and BMI.

Visceral adiposity generates leptin, TNF-alpha, and free fatty acids that independently activate keratinocyte proliferation pathways and contribute to insulin resistance. Even in the absence of frank hyperinsulinemia, adipokine-driven keratinocyte activation contributes to the lesion in morbidly obese individuals.

causes.cause: Paraneoplastic secretion of growth factors (malignant form)
causes.description: Tumors — primarily gastric adenocarcinoma, and also pancreatic, hepatic, ovarian, and breast adenocarcinomas — secrete transforming growth factor-alpha (TGF-alpha), IGF-1, and other growth factors into the systemic circulation. These ligands drive rapid and extensive epidermal hyperplasia far beyond the area of skin fold contact, explaining the mucous membrane and palmar involvement.

causes.cause: Drug effects on insulin levels or growth-factor receptors
causes.description: Corticosteroids cause secondary insulin resistance at physiological and pharmacological doses. Nicotinic acid increases insulin resistance dose-dependently. Insulin injections at high dose directly stimulate IGF-1 receptors. HIV protease inhibitors cause metabolic syndrome. All are reversible on withdrawal.

PCOS is associated with both intrinsic insulin resistance (independent of BMI) and relative hyperinsulinemia. Approximately 35-50% of women with PCOS have clinically visible acanthosis nigricans, making it a useful clinical screen. The combination of acanthosis nigricans, hirsutism, and irregular menstrual cycles in a young woman should prompt PCOS evaluation.

causes.cause: Genetic syndromes with insulin receptor mutations
causes.description: Type A extreme insulin resistance syndrome (HAIR-AN: hyperandrogenism, insulin resistance, acanthosis nigricans) involves loss-of-function mutations in the insulin receptor gene. Leprechaunism and Rabson-Mendenhall syndrome are severe receptor-pathway disorders presenting with acanthosis nigricans in infancy. These are rare but generate the most severe skin lesions.

riskFactors.description: The strongest modifiable risk factor; acanthosis nigricans prevalence rises from <10% at normal BMI to 40-70% at BMI >35 across multiple population studies. Each 5-unit increase in BMI approximately doubles the odds of acanthosis nigricans in adolescents.

Fasting hyperinsulinemia and elevated HOMA-IR (insulin resistance index) are directly associated with acanthosis nigricans severity. Approximately 66-80% of adults with type 2 diabetes and BMI >35 have visible acanthosis nigricans.

riskFactors.factor: Hispanic, African-American, or Native American ethnicity
riskFactors.description: At equivalent BMI, these populations have substantially higher acanthosis nigricans prevalence than white populations — 40-50% vs 15-20% in pediatric obesity studies — likely reflecting genetic variation in insulin signaling or baseline insulin secretory capacity.

PCOS independently causes insulin resistance in approximately 70% of affected women regardless of BMI. Acanthosis nigricans is present in 35-50% of women with PCOS and is a useful clinical screening marker.

Long-term systemic corticosteroid use causes secondary insulin resistance and acanthosis nigricans in susceptible individuals; incidence estimated 5-15% in long-term users. Nicotinic acid and HIV antiretrovirals carry similar risk.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of type 2 diabetes or insulin resistance
riskFactors.description: First-degree relatives of people with type 2 diabetes have higher rates of acanthosis nigricans at equivalent BMI, reflecting shared genetic susceptibility to hyperinsulinemia.

In non-obese adults over 40 with rapidly progressive acanthosis nigricans, underlying adenocarcinoma — particularly gastric — is the underlying cause in up to 50% of cases. This form is rare but clinically critical.

affectedDemographics: Obese children and adolescents: acanthosis nigricans is present in 35-65% of obese children, making the neck examination a routine clinical screening tool for insulin resistance in pediatric practice.
Women with PCOS: 35-50% prevalence regardless of BMI; acanthosis nigricans in a normal-weight young woman should prompt PCOS and insulin resistance evaluation.
Hispanic, African-American, and Native American populations: 2-4 times higher age-adjusted prevalence than white populations at equivalent BMI in US studies.
Adults with gastric adenocarcinoma: malignant acanthosis nigricans is rare but represents the skin in roughly 1-3% of gastric cancer cases, often preceding diagnosis.
diagnosisOverview: Acanthosis nigricans is diagnosed clinically by the characteristic appearance of dark, velvety, hyperpigmented plaques in flexural body areas. The history and clinical context immediately suggest the likely underlying cause: obesity and family diabetes history point to the metabolic form; rapid onset without obesity in a middle-aged adult with constitutional symptoms points to paraneoplastic disease. The key clinical distinction is between benign metabolic and malignant paraneoplastic forms. The metabolic workup includes fasting glucose, HbA1c, fasting insulin, HOMA-IR calculation, and lipid panel in all patients; LH:FSH ratio, free testosterone, and pelvic ultrasound in women with PCOS features; and thyroid function tests. Skin biopsy is rarely required for diagnosis but shows hyperkeratosis, papillomatosis, and increased melanin deposition in the epidermis when performed. In non-obese adults with rapid-onset acanthosis nigricans involving mucous membranes or palms (tripe palms), malignancy screening is mandatory: upper GI endoscopy or CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis combined with tumor markers (CEA, CA19-9, AFP). Dermoscopy shows a distinctive cobblestone or cerebriform pattern that helps differentiate acanthosis nigricans from other pigmented skin disorders. The condition must be distinguished from terra firma-forme dermatosis (removal with an alcohol swab), confluent and reticulated papillomatosis (Gougerot-Carteaud syndrome), and pigmented contact dermatitis.

diagnosticTests.test: Fasting glucose, HbA1c, and fasting insulin (HOMA-IR)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms insulin resistance or diabetes as the underlying metabolic cause and grades severity
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw after 8-12 hours fasting. Results available the same day. HOMA-IR is calculated from fasting glucose and insulin; values above 2.5 indicate insulin resistance.

diagnosticTests.test: Lipid panel (fasting total cholesterol, LDL, HDL, triglycerides)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for metabolic syndrome, which frequently coexists with insulin resistance–associated acanthosis nigricans
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw after 8-12 hours fasting. Results available same day in most settings.

diagnosticTests.test: Pelvic ultrasound and androgen profile (LH, FSH, free testosterone, DHEAS) in women
diagnosticTests.purpose: Diagnoses PCOS as the underlying cause when acanthosis nigricans occurs in a young woman with irregular periods or hirsutism
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 20-30 minute transvaginal or transabdominal ultrasound. Blood draw for hormones any time (day 2-5 of cycle for LH:FSH).

diagnosticTests.purpose: Characterizes the cobblestone or cerebriform surface pattern of acanthosis nigricans, supporting clinical diagnosis and differentiating from terra firma-forme dermatosis and confluent papillomatosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A handheld optical magnifier with polarized light is applied to the skin by the dermatologist; non-invasive, takes 1-2 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms the histological diagnosis in atypical or uncertain cases; shows hyperkeratosis, papillomatosis, and hyperpigmentation of the basal layer without significant inflammatory infiltrate
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 3-4 mm punch biopsy under local anaesthetic in clinic; 1-2 stitches; results in 7-10 days from dermatopathology.

diagnosticTests.test: Upper GI endoscopy and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (when malignant form suspected)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for occult adenocarcinoma in non-obese adults with rapid-onset extensive acanthosis nigricans involving mucous membranes or palms
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Endoscopy is a day procedure under light sedation; CT scan takes 30 minutes. Both are initiated urgently in the appropriate clinical context.

treatmentOverview: For the dominant metabolic form, treating the underlying cause is the most effective intervention: weight loss, insulin-sensitizing medications, and management of diabetes or PCOS produce visible improvement in acanthosis nigricans in most patients. A 5-10% reduction in body weight produces measurable lightening of the skin lesions in prospective studies. Metformin, the first-line insulin-sensitizing agent for type 2 diabetes and insulin resistance in PCOS (500-2,000 mg daily in divided doses), reduces fasting insulin and HOMA-IR; clinical trials in adolescents with obesity show improvement in neck acanthosis nigricans scores at 3-6 months. GLP-1 receptor agonists (semaglutide, liraglutide) provide weight loss and insulin sensitization that improves acanthosis nigricans as a secondary endpoint in obesity trials. Topical treatments address the cosmetic aspects of the skin lesion but do not modify the underlying metabolic driver: keratolytics (topical urea 10-40%, salicylic acid 6%, lactic acid 12%) reduce the thickened plaques; topical retinoids (tretinoin 0.025-0.1%) and topical vitamin D analogues (calcipotriol) reduce epidermal hyperplasia with regular application. Triple-combination bleaching creams (hydroquinone, tretinoin, corticosteroid) reduce hyperpigmentation but do not address the underlying pathology. Laser therapy (fractional CO2 laser, Q-switched Nd:YAG) and dermabrasion have been used for severe cosmetically distressing lesions with short-term improvement but no systemic benefit. Malignant acanthosis nigricans is treated only by effective treatment of the underlying cancer; surgical resection of the tumor typically produces partial regression of the skin lesions within weeks. Drug-induced forms resolve when the causative agent is withdrawn.

medicalTreatments.name: Metformin (500-2,000 mg daily in divided doses)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line insulin-sensitizing agent for insulin resistance–associated acanthosis nigricans. Reduces hepatic glucose production and peripheral insulin resistance, lowering fasting insulin concentrations that drive keratinocyte proliferation. Standard of care for both the metabolic management and the skin lesion in patients with insulin resistance or type 2 diabetes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improvement in acanthosis nigricans severity scores in 50-65% of adolescent patients with obesity and insulin resistance over 3-6 months in prospective trials (PMID 12634985)

medicalTreatments.name: GLP-1 receptor agonists (semaglutide 0.25-2.4 mg weekly subcutaneous; liraglutide 0.6-3 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Produce significant weight loss and insulin sensitization; indirect improvement in acanthosis nigricans as a secondary effect of reducing hyperinsulinemia. Recommended in obesity management guidelines (ADA 2024 Standards of Medical Care) for patients with BMI >30 or BMI >27 with metabolic comorbidities.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Semaglutide produces mean weight loss of 15-17% at 68 weeks (STEP 1 trial); associated with significant improvement in metabolic syndrome markers including insulin resistance

medicalTreatments.name: Topical tretinoin (0.025-0.1% cream applied nightly)
medicalTreatments.description: Retinoid that reduces epidermal hyperplasia and papillomatosis by normalizing keratinocyte differentiation. Reduces skin thickness and improves texture. Does not address hyperpigmentation significantly. Requires 12-16 weeks for visible effect; causes initial irritation and photosensitivity.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Moderate reduction in plaque thickness in small controlled trials; insufficient data for head-to-head comparisons with systemic agents

medicalTreatments.name: Topical keratolytics (urea 10-40% or lactic acid 12% or salicylic acid 6%)
medicalTreatments.description: Mechanical keratinocyte shedding reduces skin-fold plaque thickness and improves surface texture. Applied twice daily to affected areas. The simplest and most tolerated topical approach for mild to moderate disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptomatic improvement in texture and thickness in clinical series; no randomized trial data; considered standard adjunctive topical care

medicalTreatments.name: Oral contraceptives combined with metformin (in PCOS)
medicalTreatments.description: Combination treatment addresses both the hyperandrogenism and insulin resistance components of PCOS-associated acanthosis nigricans. Metformin alone is preferred when weight loss and insulin resistance are the primary goals.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Combined regimen produces greater reduction in androgen levels and HOMA-IR than either agent alone in PCOS-specific trials (Cochrane 2012, PMID 22592672)

medicalTreatments.name: Calcipotriol 0.005% topical (vitamin D3 analogue)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduces keratinocyte proliferation and promotes differentiation through vitamin D receptor signaling. Applied once to twice daily; comparable to topical tretinoin in small trials. Advantages include good tolerability and minimal irritation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improvement in plaque thickness and hyperpigmentation reported in small case series; evidence level limited to observational data

surgicalOptions.name: Fractional CO2 laser resurfacing
surgicalOptions.description: Ablative laser targeting the papillomatous epidermal surface, reducing plaque thickness and improving cosmesis in severe or refractory cases. Multiple sessions (3-5) required at 4-6 week intervals.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Clinically significant improvement in texture and thickness in 60-70% of treated patients in case series; improvements are temporary without addressing the underlying metabolic driver

surgicalOptions.name: Dermabrasion
surgicalOptions.description: Mechanical abrasion of thickened plaques in the neck and axillae under local anaesthetic. Short-term cosmetic improvement; used when laser facilities are not available.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptomatic improvement in individual cases; limited evidence from case series only

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary modification and lifestyle intervention
alternativeTreatments.description: A low-glycemic-index diet reducing postprandial insulin spikes, combined with caloric restriction and regular aerobic exercise, produces measurable reductions in fasting insulin and HOMA-IR. Meta-analyses of lifestyle intervention in overweight adolescents show significant improvement in acanthosis nigricans scores in 50-70% of compliant participants (PMID 18625662).

alternativeTreatments.name: Topical alpha-hydroxy acid preparations (glycolic acid 8-12%)
alternativeTreatments.description: Exfoliating keratolytics used in cosmetic practice for mild lesions; reduce surface pigmentation and texture without systemic effects. Well-tolerated as adjunct to metabolic management.

whySeeSpecialist: A dermatologist should evaluate acanthosis nigricans to confirm the diagnosis, distinguish benign from malignant forms, and guide topical management. An endocrinologist or metabolic physician manages the underlying insulin resistance, diabetes, or PCOS. Rapid-onset acanthosis nigricans in a non-obese adult requires urgent oncology referral.
doctorSelectionGuide: For the metabolic form, a general practitioner or endocrinologist can manage the underlying condition, with dermatology for topical treatment guidance. When the paraneoplastic form is suspected, a gastroenterologist and oncologist should be involved urgently. Pediatric endocrinology manages acanthosis nigricans in children and adolescents.
hospitalCriteria: Endocrinology or metabolic medicine clinic for insulin resistance workup and management
Dermatology clinic with dermoscopy and punch biopsy capability
Gastroenterology for upper GI endoscopy in suspected malignant form
Oncology and multidisciplinary cancer team for paraneoplastic workup
Dietitian and lifestyle modification programme access
keyFacilities: Dermatology outpatient clinics with dermoscopy and skin biopsy facilities
Paediatric endocrinology for children and adolescents
Gastroenterology and oncology services for rapid-onset non-obese cases
preventionStrategies: Achieve and maintain a healthy body weight (BMI 18.5-24.9) through diet and physical activity — weight loss of 5-10% produces visible improvement in acanthosis nigricans in prospective studies.
Follow a low-glycemic-index, high-fiber diet (vegetables, legumes, whole grains) to reduce postprandial insulin spikes and fasting hyperinsulinemia.

Screen children who are overweight or obese for insulin resistance with fasting glucose and insulin at annual well-child visits — early detection allows lifestyle intervention before type 2 diabetes develops.
Manage PCOS with evidence-based interventions (metformin, lifestyle modification) to reduce hyperinsulinemia and the risk of acanthosis nigricans development.
Review medications annually for insulin-raising agents (corticosteroids, niacin, certain antipsychotics) and substitute alternatives when acanthosis nigricans develops.
lifestyleModifications: Reduce caloric intake by 500-750 kcal/day below estimated energy expenditure — this rate of caloric deficit produces 0.5-0.75 kg/week of weight loss and measurable insulin sensitization within 4-8 weeks.
Perform 150 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic exercise per week (ADA 2024 recommendation) — exercise reduces post-meal insulin secretion independently of weight change.
Add resistance training two to three sessions per week — skeletal muscle is the primary tissue for insulin-mediated glucose disposal; greater muscle mass reduces systemic insulin levels.
Gently clean affected skin folds with mild soap daily to reduce secondary yeast colonization (Candida) in the moist crease environment.
Apply prescribed keratolytic cream (urea or lactic acid) to affected areas after bathing while the skin is still warm and slightly damp to maximize penetration.
Attend regular follow-up with the metabolic or endocrine team to monitor fasting insulin, HbA1c, and acanthosis nigricans severity as treatment response indicators.

recommended: Low glycemic index foods (legumes, non-starchy vegetables, whole grains, nuts) to blunt postprandial insulin secretion
High fiber intake (30-35 g/day) from vegetables, fruits, and whole grains to improve insulin sensitivity through gut microbiome modulation
Lean protein sources (fish, chicken, legumes, tofu) at each meal to promote satiety and reduce carbohydrate-driven insulin spikes
Omega-3-rich oily fish (salmon, sardines, mackerel, 2-3 servings/week) to reduce adipose inflammation and improve insulin sensitivity
avoid: Sugar-sweetened beverages, refined carbohydrates, and foods with added sugars — these produce the largest postprandial insulin surges
Refined white flour products (white bread, pastries, white rice) with a high glycemic index that drive hyperinsulinemia
Processed meats and saturated fat–rich foods associated with visceral adiposity and insulin resistance
Nicotinic acid (niacin) supplements in high doses (above 500 mg/day) unless medically prescribed and monitored for metabolic effects

exerciseGuidelines: At least 150 minutes of moderate aerobic exercise weekly (brisk walking, cycling, swimming) reduces fasting insulin by 10-20% independently of weight loss in adults with insulin resistance. Resistance training (two to three sessions weekly targeting major muscle groups) improves insulin-mediated glucose uptake in skeletal muscle. For children and adolescents with obesity-associated acanthosis nigricans, 60 minutes of moderate to vigorous activity daily is recommended by the American Academy of Pediatrics.
dailyManagement: Take metformin or prescribed insulin-sensitizing medication with meals to reduce gastrointestinal side effects and optimize absorption.
Apply topical tretinoin or keratolytic cream to affected areas at night after washing and drying skin folds.
Gently clean neck, axillae, and groin folds during daily bathing; use a mild, pH-balanced cleanser to reduce secondary infection.
Monitor fasting glucose or use continuous glucose monitoring if prescribed, to track metabolic response to lifestyle and pharmacological interventions.
Keep a food and exercise diary for at least 4-8 weeks when beginning lifestyle modification — objective records improve adherence and allow clinical review.
Photograph affected skin areas monthly to objectively track improvement — acanthosis nigricans changes slowly, and photographs prevent underestimation of gains.
prognosis: The prognosis of insulin resistance–associated acanthosis nigricans is directly tied to successful metabolic management. With significant weight loss (5-10% of body weight) and normalization of fasting insulin levels, most patients experience visible lightening and thinning of lesions within 3-6 months; complete resolution occurs in those who achieve and sustain normal insulin sensitivity. Without metabolic intervention, the skin lesions persist indefinitely and may progress alongside worsening insulin resistance. Acanthosis nigricans in children that resolves with lifestyle intervention is associated with lower long-term risk of type 2 diabetes compared with persistent lesions. Drug-induced acanthosis nigricans has an excellent prognosis with drug withdrawal, typically resolving within 6-12 months. Malignant acanthosis nigricans carries the prognosis of the underlying cancer — gastric adenocarcinoma at the time of skin diagnosis is typically advanced, and 5-year survival is poor (below 25%); skin lesions may partly regress with palliative chemotherapy or surgical resection.
recoveryTimeline: Visible improvement in metabolic acanthosis nigricans begins at 6-12 weeks of sustained weight loss and insulin sensitization. Substantial lightening and texture improvement by 3-6 months. Complete resolution with normal insulin levels and sustained healthy weight over 12-24 months. Drug-induced forms: improvement begins within 4-8 weeks of withdrawal, resolution in 6-12 months.
complications: Type 2 diabetes development: acanthosis nigricans is a visible predictor of progression from prediabetes to type 2 diabetes — annual HbA1c monitoring is recommended in all affected individuals.
Metabolic syndrome (hypertension, dyslipidemia, central obesity) frequently coexists and requires parallel cardiovascular risk management.

Secondary skin infection (intertrigo, candidal or bacterial colonization) in moist, thickened flexural areas — recognized by erythema, odor, and satellite pustules; treated with antifungal or antibacterial topical agents.
Psychological distress and reduced self-esteem from the cosmetically conspicuous neck and axillary lesions — documented in pediatric and adolescent populations at rates exceeding 30%.

Missed diagnosis of the malignant form — the most serious complication; gastric adenocarcinoma presenting with acanthosis nigricans has a median diagnosis-to-death interval of 12-18 months when diagnosed late.
Progression of PCOS complications (infertility, endometrial hyperplasia, cardiovascular risk) when acanthosis nigricans signals inadequately treated insulin resistance in women with PCOS.

supportResources.name: American Diabetes Association
supportResources.description: Patient resources on insulin resistance, prediabetes, and type 2 diabetes management with lifestyle and pharmacological guides relevant to acanthosis nigricans prevention and treatment.

supportResources.name: PCOS Challenge National Patient Organization
supportResources.description: US-based advocacy group for women with PCOS with resources on insulin resistance management, diet, and hormone balance — directly relevant to PCOS-associated acanthosis nigricans.

supportResources.name: British Association of Dermatologists (BAD) — Patient Information Leaflets
supportResources.description: Dermatologist-reviewed patient information on skin conditions including acanthosis nigricans, with guidance on diagnosis and treatment options.

confusedWithConditions.name: Eczema
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Eczema causes itchy, inflamed, and often weeping skin with a well-defined atopic history. Acanthosis nigricans is non-inflammatory, velvety, and persistently darker without oozing or clear atopic triggers. Dermoscopy and biopsy confirm the distinction, as does the metabolic history and distribution in skin folds.

confusedWithConditions.name: Psoriasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Psoriasis produces well-demarcated erythematous plaques with silver scale, typically on the extensor surfaces, scalp, and umbilicus. Flexural psoriasis (inverse psoriasis) can mimic acanthosis nigricans but shows a smooth red surface rather than velvety hyperpigmentation, and biopsies show neutrophilic microabscesses rather than hyperkeratosis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Atopic Dermatitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Atopic dermatitis causes eczematous, intensely pruritic skin changes with a personal or family history of atopy. Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation from atopic dermatitis can mimic acanthosis nigricans in skin folds, but the skin surface is flat rather than velvety, and the history of eczema flares distinguishes it.

confusedWithConditions.name: Melanoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Melanoma in the axilla or groin presents as an asymmetric pigmented lesion with irregular borders and evolving appearance. Acanthosis nigricans is symmetric, diffusely distributed across the fold, velvety, and non-evolving in surface pattern. Dermoscopy and biopsy resolve any uncertainty.

relatedConditions.name: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus
relatedConditions.relevance: Acanthosis nigricans is a cutaneous biomarker of the insulin resistance that precedes and accompanies type 2 diabetes; its presence should trigger diabetes screening and prevention interventions.

relatedConditions.name: Obesity
relatedConditions.relevance: Obesity drives the hyperinsulinemia that produces acanthosis nigricans; weight management is the primary treatment for the skin condition in obese individuals.

relatedConditions.name: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: PCOS is a common and frequently under-recognized cause of acanthosis nigricans in young women via intrinsic insulin resistance and hyperinsulinemia independent of BMI.

relatedConditions.name: Urticaria (Hives)
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are dermatological conditions with systemic metabolic or immunological associations; urticaria shares with acanthosis nigricans the clinical pattern of skin changes that reflect systemic disorders.

faqs.question: What is acanthosis nigricans?
faqs.answer: Acanthosis nigricans is a skin condition causing dark, velvety, thickened patches in body folds and creases — most commonly the back of the neck, armpits, and groin. It is usually a sign of high insulin levels from obesity, prediabetes, or type 2 diabetes, and is not a skin disease itself but a marker of the underlying metabolic condition.

faqs.question: What causes acanthosis nigricans?
faqs.answer: The most common cause is high insulin (hyperinsulinemia) from obesity, prediabetes, type 2 diabetes, or PCOS. Excess insulin stimulates skin cell proliferation in folds, producing the velvety dark patches. A rare form is paraneoplastic — caused by growth factors from internal cancers, especially gastric adenocarcinoma.

faqs.question: Is acanthosis nigricans a sign of diabetes?
faqs.answer: Yes, often. Acanthosis nigricans is one of the most visible external signs of insulin resistance, which precedes type 2 diabetes. About 66-80% of adults with type 2 diabetes and BMI over 35 have visible acanthosis nigricans. Its presence warrants fasting glucose, HbA1c, and fasting insulin testing.

faqs.question: Can acanthosis nigricans be cured?
faqs.answer: For the metabolic form, addressing the underlying cause effectively treats the skin: weight loss of 5-10% produces visible lightening within 3-6 months; complete resolution occurs with normalization of insulin levels. Topical treatments improve cosmesis but do not cure. Drug-induced forms resolve when the causative medication is withdrawn.

faqs.question: What does acanthosis nigricans look like?
faqs.answer: Acanthosis nigricans appears as dark brown to black, velvety or velvety-rough thickened skin in body folds. Common locations are the back of the neck, armpits, and groin. The skin may have a 'dirty' appearance that does not wash off. Skin tags frequently appear in the same areas.

faqs.question: Is acanthosis nigricans contagious?
faqs.answer: No. Acanthosis nigricans is not an infection and cannot spread to other people or to new areas of skin through contact. It is a reaction of the skin to hormonal or metabolic signals from within the body.

faqs.question: How is acanthosis nigricans treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment addresses the underlying cause. Weight loss and metformin or GLP-1 receptor agonists reduce insulin levels in metabolic forms. Topical tretinoin, urea creams, and keratolytics improve skin texture cosmetically. Topical vitamin D analogues (calcipotriol) reduce thickening. Laser therapy is used for severe cosmetically distressing lesions.

faqs.question: Can children get acanthosis nigricans?
faqs.answer: Yes. Acanthosis nigricans is increasingly common in children and adolescents with obesity, affecting 35-65% of obese children. It is one of the most important clinical signs of insulin resistance in pediatric practice and should prompt fasting glucose and insulin testing at well-child visits.

faqs.question: Is acanthosis nigricans dangerous?
faqs.answer: The skin lesion itself is not dangerous. The significance lies in what it signals: insulin resistance, prediabetes, type 2 diabetes, or PCOS in most cases. The rare malignant form is a serious paraneoplastic marker of internal cancer. Early identification of the metabolic form allows preventive intervention before diabetes develops.

faqs.question: What is malignant acanthosis nigricans?
faqs.answer: Malignant acanthosis nigricans is a paraneoplastic skin condition caused by growth factors secreted by internal cancers — most commonly gastric adenocarcinoma. It is rare but clinically urgent: rapid-onset, extensive lesions involving the mouth, palms, and soles in a non-obese adult should prompt immediate cancer screening.

faqs.question: Does weight loss improve acanthosis nigricans?
faqs.answer: Yes. Weight loss of 5-10% of body weight reduces fasting insulin levels and produces visible lightening and thinning of acanthosis nigricans lesions in 50-70% of patients within 3-6 months. Sustained weight loss leading to normal insulin sensitivity can result in complete resolution over 12-24 months.

faqs.question: Is acanthosis nigricans related to PCOS?
faqs.answer: Yes. Approximately 35-50% of women with PCOS have acanthosis nigricans from PCOS-associated insulin resistance, even at normal body weight. The combination of acanthosis nigricans, hirsutism, and irregular periods in a young woman should prompt evaluation for PCOS.

faqs.question: What ethnicities are most affected by acanthosis nigricans?
faqs.answer: Hispanic, African-American, and Native American populations have 2-4 times higher rates of acanthosis nigricans compared with white populations at equivalent BMI. These differences likely reflect genetic variation in baseline insulin secretory capacity and insulin signaling in keratinocytes.

faqs.question: Can acanthosis nigricans appear on the face?
faqs.answer: Benign metabolic acanthosis nigricans rarely involves the face. Malignant paraneoplastic acanthosis nigricans can involve the perioral skin, lips, and oral mucosa — facial and mucosal involvement in a non-obese adult is a red-flag sign requiring urgent investigation for internal malignancy.

faqs.question: What cream helps acanthosis nigricans?
faqs.answer: Topical urea cream (10-40%), lactic acid lotion (12%), or salicylic acid (6%) reduce plaque thickness and improve skin texture. Topical tretinoin (0.025-0.1%) at night reduces epidermal hyperplasia over 12-16 weeks. Calcipotriol 0.005% is a well-tolerated alternative. None remove the underlying cause — metabolic management is essential.

simpleName: Dark velvety skin patches in body folds (acanthosis nigricans)
symptomKeywords: dark skin neck does not wash off
metaDescription: Acanthosis nigricans: dark, velvety patches of skin often linked to insulin resistance. Causes, what it signals, and treatment reviewed here.
